content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO

770 L STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6732

591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

SAN FRANCISCO

650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2400
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

March 4, 1991

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Breeze:

     I am writing to request written confirmation of advice you
provided me on the telephone on February 13, 1991.

     I had made an inquiry regarding the legality and reporting
of expenses and gifts provided to public officials under the new
laws regulating gifts, honoraria and travel. (Gov. Code §§ 89501
to 89507.) My understanding of your advice is summarized below.

     Section 89502 prohibits state officials from receiving
honoraria. Subdivision (a) of section 89506, however, allows
state officials to receive free transportation to and from a
speech, along with lodging and meals for the day before, the day
of and the day after the speech. (Section 89506 refers to these
payments collectively as "travel.")

     According to our telephone conversation, it is the staff's
present opinion that any "travel" payments provided to an
official which do not fall explicitly within the exemptions
established by section 89506(a) are illegal honoraria under
section 89502.

     For instance, you advised that if a company holds a
conference and invites a state official to make a speech at the
conference, it may pay for the official's transportation to and
from the conference, along with lodging and meals at the
conference, as long as these payments are for "travel" on the day
before, the day of, or the day after the official makes the
speech. However, a ticket for an entertainment activity held on
one of the free evenings of the conference, or an extra night's
stay in the conference hotel, is, according to your analysis, an
impermissible honorarium.

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
March 4, 1991
Page 2

     For reasons discussed below, we believe your advice
construes the term "honorarium" more broadly than was intended by
the Legislature.

     The new law, pre-existing provisions of the Act and the
regulations interpreting those provisions seem to establish a
hierarchy of payments associated with a speech or appearance:

     1.   Honoraria are banned (§ 89502);

     2.   Expenses "directly in connection" with a speaking event
(e.g., absolutely necessary to the official's attendance) are
neither reportable nor subject to limits (2 CCR §§ 18623,
18728);¹

     3.   Payments, advances or reimbursements in addition to but
not including those expenses described in #2 above, which are "in
connection with" a speech, are reportable on the new travel
schedule but are not subject to limits (§ 89506(a));² and

     4.   Payments or expenses which are neither "directly in
connection with" or "in connection with" a speech are gifts which
are reportable, subject to limits and which may subject the
official to disqualification.

     Your advice suggests that payments or expenses in category #4
belong in category #1.³

     We respectfully submit that this characterization of
category #4 payments or expenses exceeds the charge of the new
statute.  Section 89502 only prohibits state officials from
accepting a "payment made in consideration for any speech given,
article published, or attendance at any public or private
conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering"; i.e., state officials may not get paid for making
speeches or appearances.  The statute does not, however, prohibit

_______________________
¹We sent a letter dated February 22, 1991 to John Wallace
seeking confirmation that these regulations apply to the new law,
and that expenses which fall within the parameters set by
regulations 18623 and 18728 are not subject to the new limits on
gifts.

²These payments, advances and reimbursements include
domestic travel and lodging and meals provided on the day of, the
day before and the day after a speech.

³See enclosed chart summarizing this list.

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
March 4, 1991
Page 3

these officials from accepting payments or expenses that are not
made "in consideration for" a speech, article, or appearance,
i.e., gifts. It simply limits the amount of these "non-
honoraria/gift" payments to $250 per year.

The history of regulations 18623 and 18728 supports the
position that hosts may defray certain expenses of public
officials directly in connection with a speech. The Commission
created exemptions for meals, accommodations, incidentals and
intrastate transportation because it concluded that the public
official's speech, appearance on a panel or participation in a
seminar constituted sufficient "consideration for" these
expenses. Under the definition of gift in section 82009, these
benefits are therefore not gifts. If the official receives
payments which exceed these regulatory parameters (for example,
accommodations beyond what the Commission felt was "necessary"
to the speaking appearance [2 CCR §§ 18728]), he or she has
received a gift (albeit not subject to the new limit) -- but not
an honorarium.

New section 87207(c) (as amended by AB 4143, Ch. 1075, 1990
session) also reflects the conclusion that payments outside the
parameters of regulations 18623 and 18728 and section 89506(a)
are gifts but not honoraria. That section provides that
reimbursements for travel which fall within the travel exemption
of section 89506 may be reported by the official on a special
"travel schedule" attached to his or her Statement of Economic
Interests; however, if he or she chooses not to use that
schedule, the filer "shall disclose payments for travel as a
gift" (or "as income," if the services provided were equal to or
greater in value than the payments for travel) -- but in any case
not as an honorarium. (Emphasis added.)5

If payments which fall outside the time frames in
regulations 18623, 18728 and section 89506(a) are banned as
honoraria, as opposed to limited as gifts, countless unnecessary

_______________________
4The FPPC has not yet developed the travel schedule referred
to in section 87207(c).

5The SEI forms designed for state elected officials (Form
721) contain separate schedules for gifts (schedule F) and
honoraria (schedule G). Presumably, the legislature was aware of
this fact when AB 4143 was passed. Yet the legislature
specifically provided that a fourth day's "travel" be reported as
"a gift" (i.e., on schedule G), not as an honorarium
(schedule F).

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
March 4, 1991
Page 4

uncertainties will result.  For instance:  how many days before
or after a speech must an official anticipate, or wait, before he
or she may legally receive even a modest gift from a host, in
order to ensure that the gift will not be considered "in
consideration for" the speech and therefore an illegal payment?

     What if the official speaks at one location, and the host
offers him or her free accommodations at another location two
days later?  Is that payment banned?  Or is it a gift, subject to
limits?

     What if the official attends an entertainment event held at
the same time as a conference, but receives the ticket for the
event weeks before appearing at the conference?  Or if the
official accepts a ticket to an event after agreeing to make a
speech because everyone in the audience is going to the event?

     For the reasons discussed, we respectfully suggest that
payments which fall outside the ambit of regulations 18623 and
18728 and section 89506(a) are:

     1.     Gifts that are subject to the limits in section 89504
and 89505;

     2.     Gifts that may cause disqualification under sections
87100 and 87103(e); but

     3.     Not honoraria subject to the ban in sections 89502 and
89503.

     Such a rule is clearly supported by the new statute.  (See
sections 89506(b) and 87207(c).)  If a state official wants to
remain after a conference for an extra day, and the host pays
that day's accommodations, the official has received a gift
subject to the $250 annual limit.  Ruling that it is an
impermissible honorarium clearly contradicts the Legislature's
decision that the payment be reported "as a gift not as an
honorarium.  The public purpose is no better served by allowing a
gift a week before an event but not on the day after the event.
The new limit of $250 per calendar year protects against any
possible undue influence.

     We respectfully request reconsideration of this advice.  We
would appreciate a written response on this issue even if you
choose not to reconsider it.

Blanca Breeze, Esq.
March 4, 1991
Page 5

Thank you very much for your consideration.

Very truly yours,

James R. Sutton

JRS/sfr

5909.01

Type of Payment                    Legal Status        Reportable?    Limited?

1.    Honoraria to state officials        impermissible       N/A            N/A
      (§ 89502)

2.    Transportation, lodging, meals      permissible         no             no
      and nominal benefits directly
      in connection with speaking event
      (2 CCR §§ 18623 & 18728)

3.    Transportation, lodging and         permissible         yes            no
      meals on day of, day before or
      day after speaking event
      (§ 89506(a))

4a.   Transportation, lodging, meals,     impermissible¹      N/A            N/A
      tickets, etc. in association
      with, but not in connection with,
      speaking event

      or

4b.   Transportation, lodging, meals,     permissible          yes            yes
      tickets, etc. in association       as a gift and
      with, but not in connection with,  subject to
      speaking event                     limits and
                                         disqualification²


¹FPPC position according to 2/13/91 advice from Bianca Breeze.

²Nielsen, Merksamer suggested position.

sections.question_synthetic: The letter challenges FPPC staff advice that payments to state officials for travel, meals, and entertainment outside explicit statutory exemptions constitute illegal honoraria, arguing instead that such payments should be classified as gifts subject to limits rather than banned outright.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming correspondence/challenge letter from a law firm (Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson, Parrinello & Mueller) requesting written confirmation and disputing FPPC staff advice. It is not an FPPC response letter. The document lacks traditional Question and Conclusion sections. The letter references oral advice from FPPC staff member Blanca Breeze and includes a summary table of the firm's proposed payment classification hierarchy. Document ID UNK-91-10393 suggests uncertain provenance.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The letter challenges FPPC staff advice that payments to state officials for travel, meals, and entertainment outside explicit statutory exemptions constitute illegal honoraria, arguing instead that such payments should be classified as gifts subject to limits rather than banned outright.

embedding.summary: This is a law firm letter to FPPC staff disputing oral advice provided on February 13, 1991 regarding the proper classification of payments to state officials under new gift and honoraria laws (Gov. Code §§ 89501-89507). The firm argues that the FPPC staff's interpretation improperly classifies non-qualifying travel payments as banned honoraria when they should instead be treated as limited gifts.
